speaker: interviewer
text: Today is February 23, 1996. The survivor is Benjamin Bender-- B-E-N-D-E-R. The interviewer is Susan Rosenblum-- R-O-S-E-N-B-L-U-M. We are in Boynton Beach, Florida, the United States of America. And the language is English. Today is February 23, 1996. The survivor is Benjamin Bender, B-EN-D-E-R. The interviewer Susan Rosenblum-- R-O-S-E-N-B-L-U-M. We are in Boynton Beach, Florida, the United States of America. And the language is English. What is your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Benjamin Bender.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-N-D-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czestochowa, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-Z-E-S-T-O-C-H-O-W-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: On April 5th, 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ajzik.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blima.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Berek.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was-- [INAUDIBLE] dealt with real estate. He was supervising houses of rich Polish landlords who were living abroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your home for me? What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were living in a-- two-bedroom and a living room apartment. It was a two-story home. It was, I think, about 10 or 15 tenants. We are a well-to-do family. I recall having a good life, beautiful childhood. My father was traveling a lot. He had some hobbies. He was playing the guitar. And he was a storyteller. Perhaps I will tell you one story. And then I will relate a little later to the story. In wintertime, when the snow was covering the ground, we were sitting around a fire stove. And he was playing guitar. And he was telling the story. One story is engraved in my memory. He mentioned a story about a pious Jew who was praying-- it was during the pogrom in  Russia-- when a Russian peasant entered his room wielding an axe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He wanted to-- to kill him. And then he said that a miracle happened at that time. The peasant turned to a stone. And this beautiful legend was haunted me through the Holocaust, and even-- even after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Shabbat like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Shabbat like in your home when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't-- we were traditional-- traditional Jews. But I wouldn't say-- I didn't pay too much, you know, to Judaism. My mother used to light candles at Friday evening. My father used to go to shul. But at the same, he was smoking cigarettes. So, it wasn't something which pointed out to he was very pious. We went to  a private school when I recall I didn't learn Hebrew in the school. It was mostly Polish was the main language, then German, and Latin. So in your progressive, modern type of Jews-- a little different, because Czestochowa had 30,000 Jews. And obviously, most of them were, I mean, I would say religious Orthodox. And people-- some percentage, a small one, were a little modern. We have two synagogues. One was conservative. And one was Orthodox, which was unusual in Poland. But this was in Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, it was a very set and short celebration of manhood. It was in the ghetto. And then I really don't have any vivid recollection of something that should leave an impact on my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your mother cooking special foods for holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well it was-- it was a Jewish-- Jewish food-- you know, the soup, and fish, and kneidlach and the kreplach. And all this [? saying ?] of Jewish food. I think we are kosher to a certain point, because we had, we have a maid, Marysia, and sometimes she was handing me food which I wasn't sure if this was kosher or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any hobbies, or sports, or activities that you'd like to pursue when you were young then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- I was-- I was a normal boy. I was-- I did-- I played soccer. It's not like you have football. But you have soccer-- European soccer. I played volleyball. I was an excellent runner-- short-distance runner. And I have some skills in drawing, which I did. And then after that, I think I was writing poetry for a short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any particular goals that you wanted to achieve before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I was a very good student. And I was really-- I wanted to learn to study, and to acquire a profession. I wanted to-- to travel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's everything about my aspiration, which I cut down very shortly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any groups or organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was very, I would say, independent. I didn't-- if I recall my-- not childhood; I was 12-- I liked to-- I didn't like to, you know, to get like orders, and the ideas to be imposed on me. I was a free thinker to a certain extent, if I can say this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages were spoken in your home, beside Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. I think the parents spoke between the Yiddish, which we didn't understand. I think my brother understood a little Yiddish, much more than myself. But I didn't know Yiddish. When I was home, I don't recall. But when they spoke sometimes between them Yiddish, just for one reason, we should not understand what they want to say. So they used Yiddish language. But Polish was the spoken language.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community like before the war in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean the community in my town?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean by how many Jews are living in the? Yes. Czestochowa had about 120,000 inhabitants, 30,000-- over 30,000 Jews, and 90,000 Poles. The city was, I would say, very anti-Semitic, very, very, very, very much. Even in normal times before the war-- I am talking about 1937 and 1938-- the Poles were smashing windows in Jewish stores or carrying anti-Semitic slogans, which they say, Jews to Palestine. I have-- I have Polish friends. I have Polish friends. Of course, I have Jewish friends. Maybe my Aryan appearance-- I was blonde with blue eyes. So I was well accepted, you know, by the Poles, calling me, they would say, [NON-ENGLISH]-- a good Jew. Czestochowa is a historical city known for the famous Shrine of Matka Boska-- the Black Madonna-- which is comparable to the Lady of Guadalupe. Every year, pilgrims by thousand used to swarm to this town, walking on their knees, bleeding to reach this sacred place of this town. Even the Pope,  I think, every year pays a visit to this place, because it's very known.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about September 1st, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's very vivid-- very much engrave in my memory. On the September 1st, 1939, the German Army smashed through the Polish defenses. And Poland, in a matter of days, went on her knees, losing her independence, which was I think in 19 years. Because in 1920, she regained independence. And then in 1939, she-- she lost the independence. So we're talking about 1920 and 1939, which is 19 years exactly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a child, I recall I was in the street watching the Germans' tanks, horses, you know, wagons-- no apprehension. As a child, I was always running after, you know, marches, you know, as a child, you know, listening to the military music. I didn't have any feelings, any forebodings what might happen. But in a matter of days, 800 Jews were executed in Czestochowa. The German went from house-to-house with a list. How do they had a list in a matter of days, I have no idea. The people who were round up mostly were doctors, engineers, writers, intellectuals, business people-- the core of the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were executed?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were executed on the spot in our town. Later on-- this is a different stage-- some were sent already camps even before the Final Solution, which took place on September 23rd, 1942. But this was before that. We are talking about 1940 already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness these executions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, then I will have to go back, if you allow me to do so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: On September 23rd, 1942, we already [INAUDIBLE] living in the ghetto surrounded by barbed wire. There was rumors about [GERMAN], what they call in German settlement in the east, which was a very vague, very unclear rumors. But then at night of September 22nd, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think this was on Yom Kippur day, and the Day of Atonement. And you asked the question of why the Final Solution happened on this very day in every place in Europe. This was done because the Germans wanted to prove to the Jews that they are rejected by  their God. And it was true, tragically, unfortunately. A 12 o'clock at night, the ghetto lit up like a Christmas tree. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They-- there was a Jewish police-- volunteers who did it. I did not know why they did it-- but they collaborated with the German hoping to save their lives. I would say to save their skin. They lost their soul. And we were ordered to takes bundles of 20 kilograms-- something like this, no more than that-- and to leave everything intact and to go to a place where there was like an old market. And this was done street-by-street, because 30,000 Jews in one day was very hard to-- to cope with this. Everything was done with a precision. Everything was planned. It was like a military operation in a way. We came to this point. Thousands of people. The market was surrounded by SS troops, by Lithuanians, and Latvians-- SS volunteers. There was a group of high-ranking officers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the one who was in charge of the selection Was an SS captain by the name Degenhart, Degenhart, D-E-G-E-N-H-A-R-T. Degen means sword in English-- Degenhart. But this was his name. He was a short guy-- custom-made uniform. He had a scar on his face; black, small eyes-- piercings eyes. Very arrogant-- wearing white gloves; holding in his right hand a short swagger stick. Everyone [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was supposed to step out and to walk about five meters for evaluation. And he was limping. So he wanted to have a good view on the person. Meanwhile, there were screams [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not real far away from adjacent buildings. And objects were flowing-- or dropping from the roofs. You have some, you know, house with three, four floors. I think the Lithuanian and the Latvian were throwing babies in-- in the street. We heard shots. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Probably they were killing people who refused maybe to leave the houses. But we didn't pay attention, because I think we are paralyzed. This was a completely kind of terror when you-- when you just-- your mind stops to think, and you turn just to listen, and to obey to orders. And I went first. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was tall. I was blonde blue eyes. This would explain to you when I started walking, he looked at me. But he look at me in way like I was looking straight into his eyes. And he look at me with a kind of astonishment. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Later, I had a very close encounter with him, which I will talk about this later.

speaker: interviewer
text: He asked me, what's your profession? I say, I am a gardener, which was-- it was really immature. But I went to school before in the ghetto. So we did some farming and-- and gardening. And I have an [NON-ENGLISH]-- a certificate-- which said gardener. He just gave a  half smile. What kind of profession is that? And he sent me to the right, which means for life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then went my brother. He passed. My father-- he was scared to death. I recall this. He was pale. He was trembling. He passed. He was tall. He was a good-looking man. My mother in the last-- in the weeks before the Final Solution, had some foreboding about what's going on. She was a very intelligent, very smart woman. And she was in very bad shape. When she approached him, he sent her to the left-- to the-- to the train on the right, to the wagons on railroad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember she look at us with a look like she said, I'm-- I'm going. Then something happened unexpectedly. A child darted from a group. The child was maybe five years old. And he was following his grandparents. He left his parents. And he followed his grandparents. And then there was a commotion for a split of a minute or second. It was the German were running after this little boy trying to catch him. Then my mother turned to the right. And after this first selection, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say about 20,000 Jews combined in a matter of one week, were sent straight to Treblinka. The Jews of Czestochowa weren't sent to Auschwitz. They were sent to Majdanek, which was also a place, and to Treblinka. So we remained intact. It was already September, end of September, in 1942. My grandparents from both sides, they were not living in the same street. All of them were sent to Treblinka. I have my grandfathers from both sides, grandmothers, aunts, uncles-- they went to Treblinka. Now, the Jews left [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were selected to different places to work-- factories, construction site. I went to a construction site to work, which was next to the monastery of the Jasna Gora-- the Hill of Light. I mentioned before about the Black Madonna. it was close to the place-- very close. Yes. This was a few hours after the-- the selection. I recall you had about 150 escorted by Lithuanians and-- and Latvians, which excelled in [? bestiality. ?] We were order to run. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, this like running one mile uphill. Some were in their 40s. Some were 35. Some were sick. Some were suffering from asthma. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the Lithuanian and the Latvian were whipping them. I was a good runner. It was no  problem for me-- my brother to a lesser degree. Many fell. And they were shot. Then we knew already that they drew a picture of what we are going to face in the future.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would just like to go back-- [CLEARING THROAT] excuse me-- and ask you about the time you spent in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the German invaded Czestochowa, for about six, seven months, we are still living in our old places. We living at the Kopernika Street 20. It was-- it was a normal life. But we were wearing the armbands with a Star of the David to distinguish us between the Poles and the Jews, because we were living in a mixed-- [INAUDIBLE] community. And then I would say in the middle of 1940, then-- maybe the end of 1940, we went to live in the ghetto. And the Final Solution started already when we were living in the ghetto. So I would say from 19-- end of 1940 to 1942. About two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to take a tape break now.
